TSMC has raised its 2030 global semiconductor market forecast by 50% to over $1.5 trillion, driven primarily by AI and high-performance computing demand expected to capture 55% of the market. The company projects AI accelerator wafer demand will surge elevenfold from 2022 to 2026, while CoWoS advanced packaging capacity grows at more than 80% annually through 2027. To meet this demand, TSMC plans nine new fab and packaging phases in 2026 and is accelerating international expansion in Arizona, Japan, and Germany. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) (TSM) delivered one of the most aggressive long-term forecasts in its history on Thursday, saying the global semiconductor market will surpass $1.5 trillion by 2030. TSMC Sees AI Creating a $1.5 Trillion Semiconductor Market by 2030 | YourStory. # TSMC Sees AI Creating a $1.5 Trillion Semiconductor Market by 2030. ## TSMC says the global semiconductor market could cross $1.5 trillion by 2030 as AI drives demand for advanced chips and packaging. Artificial intelligence is driving a new chip boom, and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) says the biggest wave of growth is still ahead. TSMC, the world’s largest contract chipmaker, now anticipates the global semiconductor market to surpass $1.5 trillion by 2030, sharply raising its earlier forecast of around $1 trillion. The revised projection, revealed ahead of the company’s technology symposium in May 2026, reflects surging demand for AI infrastructure, high-performance computing, and advanced chip packaging. The world's largest contract chipmaker, TSMC has revised its 2030 market projection upward by 50%, from just over $1 trillion to $1.5 trillion in the space of roughly two years. Kevin Zhang, senior vice president and deputy co-COO at TSMC, delivered the revised outlook at the company’s North America Technology, where he made a point that deserves more attention than it has received: the AI and HPC share of that market alone, 55%, or roughly $825 billion would be larger than the entire global semiconductor market today. This represents a fundamental reordering of what the chip industry exists to serve, and it carries consequences that reach well beyond the fabs in Hsinchu and Phoenix.
